summary: DHH argues that the software development community has an unhealthy reluctance to acknowledge differences in talent and competence among developers. He draws parallels to sports and medicine, where skill hierarchies are openly accepted, and contends that the lack of formal barriers to entry in programming has led people to wrongly conclude that anyone can reach the highest levels with enough effort. He criticizes top developers for downplaying their own achievements out of false humility, arguing this helps nobody. The post concludes that easy entry to the field offers a chance at pursuing competence, not a guarantee of it.
key_points: Software development lacks formal gatekeeping exams, which is good, but leads to a false belief that anyone can reach the top with enough effort
The best developers tend to downplay their talent out of false humility, unlike top athletes or surgeons who embrace their mastery
Celebrating exceptional talent attracts new people to a field rather than discouraging them, as seen in soccer with Messi and Ronaldo
There is an insecurity in the software community about whether being better even matters, which DHH calls nonsense
Low barriers to entry afford a chance at pursuing competence, not its inevitability
money_quotes: Many software developers seem to have a uniquely hard time accepting that not everyone who just tries real hard will become so good as to be among the best in this field.
The mistake comes from inferring that because there's no barrier to entry, it's within the capacity of anyone who keeps at it to ascent to the highest levels of competence possible.

In our eagerness to appear as welcoming and encouraging to the next generation of developers, we've often accidentally or intentionally debase the achievements of merit, and in the process help nobody.
More kids start playing soccer because we cherish the unique talents of Messi or Ronaldo, not fewer.
You don't see Messi or Ronaldo attribute their wins to 'just getting lucky' as a matter of course.
It reveals an unappealing insecurity about whether software development is domain where being better is even important.
What the easy entry to software development affords is a chance at the pursuit of competence, not it's inevitability.
Little worth having is not hard to get.
key_insight: The software industry's reluctance to acknowledge talent differences, combined with top developers' false humility, undermines the meritocratic promise that low barriers to entry are supposed to provide.
